(**CSA**) is a [United States Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Management_and_Budget "United States Office of Management and Budget") (OMB) term for a combination of adjacent [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") (MSA) and [micropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area") (μSA) across the 50 [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and the territory of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") that can demonstrate economic or social linkage. CSAs were first designated in 2003.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) OMB defines a CSA by various combinations of adjacent metropolitan and micropolitan areas with economic ties measured by commuting patterns. CSAs retain their own designations as metropolitan or micropolitan statistical areas in their respective larger combined statistical areas.

**Combined statistical area** (**CSA**) is a [United States Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/United_States_Office_of_Management_and_Budget "United States Office of Management and Budget") (OMB) term for a combination of adjacent [metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") (MSA) and [micropolitan statistical areas](/wiki/Micropolitan_statistical_area "Micropolitan statistical area") (μSA) across the 50 [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") and the territory of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") that can demonstrate economic or social linkage. CSAs were first designated in 2003.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) OMB defines a CSA by various combinations of adjacent metropolitan and micropolitan areas with economic ties measured by commuting patterns. CSAs retain their own designations as metropolitan or micropolitan statistical areas in their respective larger combined statistical areas.

The primary distinguishing factor between a CSA and an [MSA/μSA](/wiki/List_of_core-based_statistical_areas "List of core-based statistical areas") is that the social and economic ties between the individual MSAs/μSAs within a CSA are at lower levels than those between the counties within an MSA.[[3]](#cite_note-OMB_23-01-3) CSAs represent multiple metropolitan or micropolitan areas that have an employment interchange of at least 15% (% commuting from A to B plus % commuting from B to A).[[3]](#cite_note-OMB_23-01-3) CSAs often represent regions with overlapping labor and media markets.

As of 2023, there are 184 combined statistical areas across the United States, including three in the territory of Puerto Rico.[[3]](#cite_note-OMB_23-01-3)
